title: Chapter 35: Every Fire Needs Fuel
content: The Great Hall had become a theater of the damned, and every seat was taken.

Guests clustered in frantic islands across the ballroom floor — some jabbing at phones with shaking fingers, desperate to reach lawyers who couldn't drive through a blizzard; others pressing against the entrance doors as if willpower alone might part the wall of snow outside. A woman in midnight-blue silk sat on the floor near the stage, her gown pooled around her like spilled ink, staring at nothing. Two men in matching corsages argued in consonant hisses near the bar, their voices too low to hear over the ambient roar of collective panic.

The emergency lights had kicked on when the main power flickered — amber, sulfurous, turning the gilded walls the color of old bruises. Shadows pooled in corners where the crystal chandeliers used to cast diamonds. The chandeliers were dark now, their crystals dull as dead eyes.

I moved through the chaos in my red dress, and people parted for me. Not out of respect. Out of something closer to superstition. Like I was a bad omen that had crawled out of their worst predictions and was now walking among them, proven right.
Good. Let them move.
There were still threads to cut. Sloane first. Then I'd find Heath. Then we'd see what was left to burn.

The champagne tower alcove was easy to find — I just followed the sound of shattered glass. Someone had toppled the entire structure, hundreds of crystal flutes cascading in a domino run of destruction that left the corner glittering with sharp, wet debris. The alcohol smell was sharp enough to sting my eyes, or maybe that was something else.

Sloane sat on the floor behind the ruins of the tower, her back against the wall, her knees drawn up. Her dress was the same pale ice-blue as before, but now there was a dark stain spreading across the skirt — champagne, probably, though in the amber light it looked like something worse. Her hair had come half-undone, platinum strands stuck to her neck. She looked up when my shadow fell across her.

The hatred in her eyes was so pure it was almost beautiful. Undiluted. Undiluted by confusion, by doubt, by the social calculations that usually moderated everything Sloane Ashworth expressed. This was the raw thing underneath — the animal that had always lived behind her perfect smile, now uncaged and staring at me with bared teeth.
"You did this." Her voice was hoarse, like she'd been screaming. Maybe she had been. "You and that scholarship trash."
I didn't flinch. I'd earned this moment. I could stand in it.
"Your father did this." I kept my voice level. Almost gentle. "I just made sure everyone knew."

Something happened to Sloane's face that I hadn't expected. I'd braced for rage — for the venomous counterattack, the Sloane who always had a sharper weapon, a crueler word, a lower blow. I'd steeled myself for the girl who'd made my life at Blackwood a curated hell.
Instead, her face crumpled.
Not with rage. With grief.

It was worse. So much worse than rage. Rage I could meet. Rage I understood. But this — the collapse of her features, the way her mouth pulled inward, the way her eyes went liquid and lost — this was a girl watching her entire world end, and I was the one standing over the ashes.

Her family. Gone. Her father arrested on the floor above — I'd heard the commotion when the state police came through, felt a vicious spike of satisfaction that lasted exactly as long as it took to see a girl my age learn her father was being taken away in handcuffs. Her future — every connection, every door that the Ashworth name could open, every golden promise that came with being born on the right side of the right line — all of it, ash. And the girl in the red dress was standing over her like it was Tuesday.
Because for me, it was.

That was the part that made my stomach clench. I'd planned this. I'd executed this. And when it worked — when Sloane's world caught fire the way mine had — I felt something that wasn't quite triumph but wasn't quite horror either. Something in between. Something hungry and sick.

Sloane wiped her face with the back of her hand, smearing mascara across her cheekbone. When she looked at me again, the hatred was still there, but it had been joined by something else. Something that made my pulse stutter.
"Remember what I told you in the bathroom?" she said. Her voice was quiet now. Almost intimate. The way you speak to someone you're about to bury. "About the cage?"

I remembered. The night of the masque. The bathroom with its marble and its soft, murderous lighting. Sloane had told me I was in a cage — that we both were. That the system was the cage, and the cage was all there was.
"You thought you were escaping the cage." She laughed — a single, broken sound. "But you just built a bigger one. Out of fire and ruins. And you're going to be alone in it."
I opened my mouth.
Nothing came out.

Because Sloane might be right. And I couldn't — I couldn't stand here in my red dress, in the ruins I'd made, and tell her she was wrong. I'd burned down the walls only to find myself inside a different enclosure, one I'd constructed myself, brick by deliberate brick. Every enemy I'd made, every bridge I'd burned, every person I'd used and discarded — they were the bars now. And I was locked inside with what I'd done.
Sloane watched me struggle. Watched me fail to answer. And something shifted in her expression — not satisfaction exactly, but the grim recognition of someone watching a prediction come true.
"You think you're free," she whispered. "But you're just the fire. And fire doesn't get to leave the burning building. It dies with it."
I left her there. Not because I had a rebuttal. Because I didn't.

The Great Hall had shifted in the minutes I'd been in the alcove. The clusters of panic had thinned, reorganized. Some people had retreated to the edges, pressing against walls as if the perimeter might offer an exit that didn't exist. Others had found drinks and were pouring them down like water, as if the alcohol could sand down the edges of reality. The emergency lights pulsed — amber, amber, amber — a heartbeat made of warning.
And across the hall, Heath was working.

I spotted him near the eastern corridor, in the margin of space between the bar and the grandfather clock. He had an audience — not the crowd kind. The singular kind. A board member whose name I didn't know, a man in a charcoal suit with silver at his temples and blood draining from his face.
Heath was holding a folder.

I drifted closer, not close enough to intrude, but close enough to watch. This was his operation. His precision. I'd seen the folders earlier, of course — thick manila envelopes he'd prepared with the patient meticulousness of a surgeon laying out instruments. Each one labeled. Each one containing exactly enough evidence to destroy one specific person.

He wasn't dramatic. That was the thing about Heath. He didn't need to be. Drama required uncertainty, and Heath had none. He knew what was in each folder the way a priest knows scripture — intimately, completely, with the absolute conviction of someone for whom the evidence was sacred.

The silver-haired man reached for the folder with trembling fingers. Heath handed it over. Their hands didn't touch. A small mercy, or maybe just efficiency — Heath didn't waste anything, not even contact.

The man opened it. I watched his face cycle through the stages: confusion, recognition, disbelief, horror. Color drained from his already-pale features, then rushed back in a mottled flush. His mouth opened, closed, opened again. No sound came out.
Heath said something to him — too quiet for me to hear. It didn't matter what the words were. The folder had spoken for him. The folder always spoke for him.
The man turned and walked away, unsteady, the folder clutched against his chest like a wound he was trying to hold closed. Heath was already turning to the next one.

A woman this time. Dark hair, sharp features, emerald earrings that caught the amber light and turned it sickly. She saw him coming and her chin lifted — defiance, I thought, until I saw her hands clasped in front of her. White-knuckled. She knew what was coming.
Folder number two.

The same surgical delivery. The same quiet words. The same devastating precision. The chairman who had been paraded in front of donors as the Mexican success story — the prop, the token, the evidence that Blackwood's doors were open to anyone — was now dismantling the institution that had made him a prop. One folder at a time. One bullet at a time.
And each bullet hit its mark.

I watched three more conversations. Each one ended the same way: a board member walking away destroyed, carrying their own indictment like a corpse they couldn't put down. Heath never raised his voice. Never gestured dramatically. Never needed to. The evidence was the weapon; he was just the hand that placed it.
It should have felt good. It should have felt like justice, like vindication, like every poem about wrongs made right.
It felt like watching a surgeon operate without anesthesia. Necessary. Precise. And somehow still brutal.
I turned away and surveyed the room — really surveyed it, the way you look at something when you're trying to see the whole picture, not just the parts you want.

The board was broken. I could see the fractures running through the room like fault lines — clusters of people who'd been allies an hour ago now standing apart, avoiding eye contact, each one wondering which of their secrets had been in which folder. Robert was neutralized, sidelined in whatever corner he'd retreated to, his authority evaporated like the champagne soaking into the floor. The evidence was public — sent to every media outlet, every regulatory body, every curious blogger with a platform. It was done.

Something moved through me. Something that, if I was generous, I might call victory. It was a fragile feeling, light and tentative, like a soap bubble floating over a field of glass. But it was there — the warm, golden certainty that we'd done it. That the enemy was broken and the war was won.
I was still holding that feeling when I saw the fire alarm.

It was mounted on the eastern wall, between two emergency exits. Not pulled — the handle was still in its cradle. But the light on top was blinking red. Steady. Rhythmic. A heartbeat that didn't match the amber pulse of the emergency lights.
That wasn't right. Fire alarms didn't blink unless—
I turned toward the windows. The eastern windows. The ones that faced the east wing.
Orange.

Not the amber of emergency lighting. Not the warm glow of candlelight. This was the deep, living orange of something feeding. Something growing. Something that had teeth and appetite and was eating its way through wood and wallpaper and a hundred years of Blackwood history.
The east wing was on fire.
Not metaphorical. Not symbolic. Real fire — the kind that kills, the kind that doesn't care about evidence or revenge or careful plans. The kind that just burns.

One of the board members. It had to be. Some desperate, cornered animal who'd decided that if they were going down, they were taking the building with them. Destroy the evidence, destroy the records, destroy everything. The fire as a last resort — the ultimate delete key.
The alarm screamed.

It was a sound that cut through everything — through the murmur of shock, through the distant conversations, through the ambient dread that had settled over the room like fog. One moment the hall was a tapestry of controlled chaos; the next, it was pandemonium.

People surged toward the doors. The same doors that led to a blizzard that wouldn't let them leave. The same doors that had been holding them hostage all evening. Now the building itself was turning against them, and the exit was a wall of white nothing, and the fire was—
The sprinkler system kicked in.

Water screamed from the ceiling — cold, relentless, bursting from nozzles I'd never noticed in a deluge that turned the Great Hall into a rainstorm. It hit the shattered glass on the floor and sent fragments skittering. It hit the guests and their designer clothes and their desperate faces, and suddenly everyone was wet and screaming and the amber emergency lights were refracting through the downpour in a thousand broken beams.

I stood in it. Let it hit me. The red dress turned dark, heavy, plastered to my skin like a second skeleton. Water ran down my face and into my eyes and I didn't blink. I was watching the window. The orange glow. Growing.
*We started a fire we can't control. And now it's coming for all of us.*
Sloane's voice in my head, uninvited: *You just built a bigger one. Out of fire and ruins.*

The smoke was visible now — seeping under the eastern doors, thin and gray and insidious. The sprinklers couldn't reach the east wing. The fire was feeding on wood and paint and century-old paneling, and the water raining down on the Great Hall was doing nothing to stop it. We were drowning in one room while the next room burned, and there was no version of this that ended with us winning.
I turned, scanning the chaos. Faces I didn't recognize. Bodies pushing toward exits that wouldn't open. Water and smoke and glass and screaming.
Heath. Where was Heath?
The last time I'd seen him, he'd been near the eastern corridor. The corridor that led to the east wing. The wing that was on fire.

I pushed through the crowd. A man's shoulder caught my collarbone and I didn't feel it. A woman grabbed my arm and said something I couldn't hear over the alarm and I shook her off without looking. The water was in my eyes, in my mouth, cold and tasting of pipe metal and smoke.

The eastern corridor was obscured. Not just by people — by smoke. Thick now, rolling under the door like something alive, something hunting. The orange glow pulsed through the gaps, painting the wet floor in streaks of living color.
"Heath!" My voice vanished into the alarm, the screaming, the hiss of water. I tried again, louder, and my throat burned from smoke I'd already inhaled without noticing. "Heath!"

The crowd pushed me sideways. I caught myself against a table, grabbed the edge, steadied. The room was a watercolor of panic — colors bleeding into each other, shapes losing their edges, everything running.
I couldn't see him.
I couldn't see him anywhere.

The fire alarm screamed. The sprinklers rained. The smoke curled through the Great Hall like fingers reaching for the chandeliers, and somewhere behind the eastern doors, Blackwood Academy was burning, and the boy I'd started this fire with was somewhere on the other side of the flames.
*We started a fire we can't control.*
The water poured down, and the smoke poured in, and I couldn't see him, and Sloane's prophecy echoed in the hollow space where victory had been:
*And you're going to be alone in it.*
The fire was real now.

The destruction we'd planned had become something we couldn't contain — something with its own hunger, its own logic, its own direction. We'd thought we were the ones holding the matches. We'd thought we could choose what burned and what was spared.
We were wrong.
